title: REDESIGNING PUBLIC SECTOR WORK CULTURE: EVALUATING THE INFLUENCE OF WORK FROM ANYWHERE (WFA) POLICY ON HIGH-CALIBER TALENT ENGAGEMENT IN GOVERNMENT AGENCIES
author: Pisit Potjanajaruwit, Chutima Klaysung, Nattapong Techarattanased and Krisakorn Sukavejworakit
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: In this paper, we examine the impact of the Work from Anywhere (WFA) policy on attracting top talents to civil service using the Department of International Trade Negotiation (DITN) as a case. The methodology is mixed methods, including quantitative research (questionnaire of 62 civil servants) and qualitative research method based on in-depth interviews with five key informants. The study aimed (1) to investigate the impact of WFA policy on high performers’ choice to join civil service in DITN, and (2) to provide policy implications on human resource planning by attracting high-potential individuals joining the department. The results of the quantitative analyses demonstrated that adopting a WFA policy exerted a statistically positive impact on the propensity for high performers in entering into civil service. The strongest influence on decision-making was work–life balance at the.01 level of significance (β = 0.38, t = 11.14, p <. 001). Organizational image then followed (      β       = 0.21, t = 4.10, p <. 001), lower commuting expenses (      β       = 0.19, t = 7.70, p <. 001), and management support (      β       = 0.16, t = 2.60, p =. 013). The least influential, yet still a significant factor was the work culture in an organization (β = 0.12, t = 2.70, p =. 008).

keywords: Flexible Work Arrangements, High-Performing Individuals, Organizational Image and Work Culture, Talent Attraction in Public Sector, Work from Anywhere (WFA) Policy.
